title: Was Marx Right? A 60-Minute Self-Study on Marx’s Analysis of Capitalism

sections.title: 1. Orientation: What Does ‘Was Marx Right?’ Mean?
sections.content: This session explores whether Karl Marx’s analysis of capitalism remains valid today. Rather than asking for a simple yes/no answer, we evaluate Marx’s key claims about how capitalism works and compare them with contemporary economic realities. You should aim to understand Marx’s original arguments, how they were intended to explain 19th-century capitalism, and how they might apply—or fail to apply—to the 21st century. Focus on interpreting concepts, assessing evidence, and recognising competing perspectives.

sections.title: 2. Core Features of Marx’s Theory
sections.content: Marx viewed capitalism as a system defined by private ownership of the means of production, wage labour, and profit-driven production. He argued that economic relations shape society (historical materialism), and that class struggle between the bourgeoisie (owners) and proletariat (workers) drives social change. Central to his critique is the idea that capitalism generates inequality and instability due to its internal dynamics, rather than external shocks.

sections.title: 3. Key Mechanisms in Marx’s Critique
sections.content: Marx identified several mechanisms within capitalism: (1) exploitation, where workers produce more value than they receive in wages (surplus value); (2) alienation, where workers become disconnected from their work, the product, and themselves; (3) accumulation and concentration of capital, leading to increasing inequality; and (4) crises of overproduction, where goods cannot be sold profitably. These mechanisms are meant to explain both everyday economic relations and long-term systemic outcomes.

sections.title: 4. Historical Outcomes: Did Marx’s Predictions Come True?
sections.content: Marx predicted that capitalism would become increasingly unequal, that the working class would become more impoverished, and that crises would intensify, potentially leading to systemic collapse. While inequality has persisted and even increased in some contexts, living standards have also improved in many countries. Capitalism has proven adaptable, incorporating reforms such as welfare states, labour rights, and regulation. Revolutions did occur in some societies, but not in the advanced capitalist countries Marx expected.

sections.title: 5. Contemporary Relevance
sections.content: Many scholars argue that Marx’s ideas remain relevant when analysing global capitalism. Issues such as wealth inequality, precarious work, global supply chains, and financial crises (e.g., 2008) can be interpreted using Marxist concepts. Digital capitalism and platform economies raise new questions about ownership and exploitation. However, critics argue that Marx underestimated capitalism’s flexibility, the role of institutions, and the importance of non-economic factors such as culture and politics.

sections.title: 6. Competing Interpretations and Evaluation
sections.content: There is no single answer to whether Marx was right. Some economists view his work as a powerful critique of inequality and instability but reject his predictions of collapse. Others see Marx as providing a useful analytical framework rather than a predictive model. Evaluating Marx involves distinguishing between his diagnosis of capitalism’s problems and his expectations about its future. A balanced view considers both his insights and his limitations.

examples: The 2008 global financial crisis can be interpreted as a Marxian crisis of overproduction and instability in financial capitalism.
The rise of gig economy platforms (e.g., ride-sharing apps) illustrates debates about worker exploitation and precarious labour.
Increasing wealth concentration among the top 1% reflects Marx’s idea of capital accumulation and inequality.
Welfare state policies in post-war Europe demonstrate capitalism’s capacity to adapt and mitigate class tensions.
Global supply chains show how labour is organised across borders, raising questions about exploitation in new contexts.
